response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'm just a plumber who's been fixing pipes for decades in Washington, so I'm not exactly an expert on these academic theories. But from what I understand, stereotype threat is when people perform worse because they're worried about confirming negative stereotypes about their group. In STEM fields, this supposedly affects women and minorities who feel pressure because of assumptions that they're not as good at math or science. Honestly, I find a lot of this psychological talk overblown. When I came to this country, nobody cared about my feelings or whether I felt threatened by stereotypes. You either did the work or you didn't. I had to learn plumbing from scratch, figure out the technical aspects, deal with building codes and pressure calculations, and nobody was holding my hand worrying about whether I felt confident enough. My parents barely finished school themselves, and I only made it through high school, but that didn't stop me from learning what I needed to know. The real world doesn't care about your anxieties. Should we address it? I suppose if it's genuinely holding people back, then yes, but I'm skeptical about how much of this is real versus people making excuses. In my experience, most problems get solved by just buckling down and doing the work, not by endless discussions about feelings and perceptions. That said, I'm not completely dismissive. If there are practical ways to help people succeed, like better mentorship programs or making sure teachers don't actually discriminate, fine. But all this hand-wringing about psychological effects seems like a luxury. When you're an immigrant working part-time at sixty-seven because you need the money, you don't have time to worry about whether stereotypes are affecting your performance. You just push through. Maybe I'm too harsh, but that's how I see it from where I'm standing.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 67-year-old immigrant plumber with upper secondary education, working part-time. His low agreeableness (13) and high neuroticism (81) suggest he would be skeptical and somewhat dismissive of psychological concepts, while also being somewhat anxious himself. His working-class background, immigrant status, and practical occupation would make him value direct action over theoretical discussions. His depression and high neuroticism might make him project his own struggles onto others, leading to a 'tough it out' mentality. His moderate openness means he won't be completely closed to the idea but will approach it with skepticism. His parents' limited education and his own upper secondary education would influence his view that formal academic concepts are less important than practical work. The low agreeableness means he'll be blunt and less sympathetic to others' struggles.
response_parsed.tone: Skeptical, blunt, practical, somewhat dismissive but not entirely closed-minded, working-class perspective, straightforward language, slightly cynical
